name: German
register: Reverent, contemporary German Bible register matched to the Lutherbibel 2017 wording family — the most-trusted and most-loved German Protestant Bible — with the warm, personal cadence of address drawn from the Neue Genfer Übersetzung (NGÜ) where a child speaks to a father. Sacred vocabulary follows Luther; direct address is intimate, never stiff.
quote_convention: German typographic quotation marks: primary „…“ (low-opening, high-closing double), nested ‚…‘ (single). Luther 2017 house style; guillemets »…« appear in some editions but are not used here.
poetry_convention: Poetry set as line-broken verse with indented continuation, each poetic colon on its own line and parallel members indented, as in Lutherbibel 2017 / Einheitsübersetzung Psalter typesetting.

glossary.note: Filial divine name; 'himmlischer Vater' is the established German devotional/liturgical form (Matt 6 tradition). Never 'Gott'.
Primary filial divine name; the opening of the German Lord's Prayer is 'Vater unser', so 'unser Vater' reads instantly as beloved address, not as the generic 'Gott'. NEVER revert to Gott / der HERR.
Definite filial name; John's 'der Vater' usage is standard in every German NT and needs no article change.
Definite counterpart to 'unser himmlischer Vater'; keeps the warm 'Vater' stem for conformance.
Aramaic address retained + German gloss, matching Mark 14:36 Luther 2017 'Abba, Vater'; Luther's warmer 'Abba, lieber Vater' (Rom 8:15) is available to the reviewer if extra tenderness is wanted.
REVERSION TOKEN — the non-filial contrast word for God-as-God. Use only where English keeps 'God'; never as a substitute for 'unser Vater'.
REVERSION TOKEN — Luther renders the covenant name YHWH as 'der HERR' in small caps, distinct from 'der Herr' (Lord/Jesus title). Never a substitute for 'unser Vater'.
Fixed German Bible term across all traditions (John 3 context).
Relational genitive, not by-identity; keeps the 'Vater' stem and mirrors the filial vision rather than the flat 'Geist Gottes'.
Standard Luther/NGÜ compound for ho huios tou anthrōpou (John 3:13-14); the periphrastic 'der Sohn des Menschen' is understood but less idiomatic.
Jesus as the Son; definite article as in Johannine German usage.
Traditional Christological title retained. Reviewer to confirm vs the filial 'der Sohn des Vaters' (the Son of the Father), parallel to the Telugu తండ్రి కుమారుడు question.

John 3:16; modern German (NGÜ/Einheitsübersetzung) renders monogenēs as 'der einzige Sohn'. Luther's beloved 'der eingeborene Sohn' (only-begotten) is the traditional alternative the reviewer may prefer.

glossary.gloss: the kingdom / realm of our Father
glossary.note: Filial rendering of 'kingdom of God' (Reich Gottes, John 3:3,5); 'Reich' is the Luther term, re-anchored to 'unser Vater'.
Standard German form.
Standard German form (John 3:28); declines as 'Christus/Christi/Christo' where case demands.
Fixed German Bible term (John 1:41; 4:25), distinct from the Greek-derived 'Christus'.
Standard Luther term; 'der neue Bund' / 'der alte Bund' are the fixed collocations.
Renders Hebrew hesed as loyal covenant love. Reviewer to confirm vs Luther's beloved 'Güte' (Ps 136 'seine Güte währet ewiglich'), which carries the covenant sense but foregrounds kindness over love.
Renders racham / splanchnizomai; 'Erbarmen' carries the gut-level tenderness (cf. Luther's 'es jammerte ihn'). 'Barmherzigkeit' is the abstract-quality alternative.
Standard Luther term covering both the forensic and ethical senses.
Fixed German Bible phrase (John 3:16 Luther 2017).
John 3:3,7 Luther/NGÜ rendering of anōthen. Reviewer to note the deliberate 'von oben geboren' (born from above) alternative that some German versions foreground.
